title: Server-side requests should not be vulnerable to traversing attacks
description: Path Traversal Server-Side Request Forgery is a sub-class of Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF). In this type of attack, the attacker manipulates the path of the URL in the server-side request, rather than controlling the entire URL. This can lead to unauthorized access to endpoints or data by altering the request path.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

triggering_conditions.description: URL construction using string interpolation, concatenation, or formatting with user input AND no path validation/sanitization
triggering_conditions.logic: Custom function detects vulnerable URL construction patterns and checks for mitigation

exclusion_conditions.description: Logging, testing, comments, ToString methods, and methods with path validation
exclusion_conditions.handled_by: Custom function with comprehensive exclusion logic

never_trigger_on: String formatting in logs ($"", string.Format for display)
Methods with explicit path validation (Contains(".."), Path.GetFileName)
logic.reasoning: Detects server-side request forgery (SSRF) path traversal vulnerabilities by identifying URL construction patterns that concatenate user input without validation. Focuses on HTTP client calls with string interpolation, Format, or concatenation. Excludes safe patterns like Path.GetFileName, allowlist validation, and traversal sequence checks. Prevents false positives in logging, testing, and properly validated code.

should_trigger:
var url = $"https://api.com/{userPath}";
return await new HttpClient().GetStringAsync(url);

var endpoint = string.Format("https://files.com/{0}", fileName);
return await client.GetAsync(endpoint);

var fullUrl = "https://docs.internal/" + documentPath;
var response = await httpClient.GetAsync(fullUrl);
should_not_trigger:
if (path.Contains("..")) throw new Exception();
var url = $"https://api.com/{path}";
var url = "https://api.com/status"; // Hard-coded, no user input
